text.title: Terraform Project Structure
text.summary: Directory layout patterns, file naming conventions, workspace strategies, and their isolation trade-offs.

rule.statement: Organize Terraform projects as environments/dev|staging|prod (each with its own backend) plus modules/ for reusable components and global/ for account-wide resources (IAM, DNS).
rule.rationale: Environment directories with separate backends provide full blast-radius isolation — a destroy in dev cannot affect prod state. Shared backend workspaces do not provide this isolation.

rule.statement: Within each environment directory, further split by component: networking/, compute/, database/. Each component directory has its own backend key and state file.
rule.rationale: Monolithic per-environment state files mean a change to a single security group requires locking all compute and database resources. Component separation limits apply scope.

section.title: Workspace Strategy

rule.statement: Use directory-per-environment over CLI workspaces for production systems; use workspaces only for lightweight variations of the same infrastructure (e.g., feature branch ephemeral environments).
rule.rationale: Workspaces share backend configuration and provider credentials. A misconfigured workspace apply can target the wrong environment. Directory isolation uses separate backend keys making cross-env accidents structurally impossible.

section.note: Workspace limitations: share backend config, share provider configuration, not suitable for system decomposition across components, insufficient blast-radius isolation for critical production systems.

rule.statement: Use the standard file set: versions.tf (version constraints), providers.tf (provider config), main.tf (primary resources), variables.tf, outputs.tf, locals.tf, data.tf, backend.tf.
rule.rationale: Consistent file layout allows any team member to navigate an unfamiliar module. main.tf is the first place engineers look for resource definitions.

rule.statement: Modules must follow single responsibility — one logical component per module; no hardcoded values; parameterize everything via variables with sensible defaults; version modules with semantic versioning tags.
rule.rationale: Single-responsibility modules are reusable without modification. Hardcoded values (AMI IDs, CIDR blocks, account IDs) make modules non-portable across environments and regions.

antipattern.description: Single monolithic Terraform configuration for all environments and components.
antipattern.reason: A single state file covering all environments means terraform plan locks all environments simultaneously. A single plan error blocks deployments to all environments.
